After thirty-eight years of tedious living in the wilderness, the armies of Israel advanced toward Canaan again. There was no water for the people. We live in a world where there is lack, and wherever we are, we must expect to encounter something that will trouble us. It is a great mercy to have plenty of water, a mercy which, if we experienced its absence, we would more fully appreciate. Because of this, they murmured against Moses and Aaron. They spoke the same absurd and foolish language their fathers had done. Their crime was made worse by the fact that they had suffered for so long because of the discontent and distrusts of their fathers, yet they proceeded in the same way. Moses must again, in God’s name, command water from a rock for them; God is as able as ever to supply his people with what is needed. But Moses and Aaron acted wrongly. They took too much of the glory of this wondrous work to themselves; “Must we fetch water?” As if it were done by some power or worthiness of their own. They were to speak to the rock, but they struck it. Therefore, it is charged against them that they did not honor God, meaning they did not give to him alone the glory of this miracle which was due to his name. And being provoked by the people, Moses spoke unadvisedly with his lips. The same pride of man would still try to take over the office of the appointed Mediator and become to ourselves wisdom, righteousness, sanctification, and redemption. Such a state of sinful independence, such a rebellion of the soul against its Savior, God condemns in every page of the gospel.

The closest way to Canaan from where Israel encamped was through the country of Edom. The ambassadors who were sent returned with a denial. The Edomites feared receiving damage from the Israelites. If this numerous army had been under any other discipline than that of the righteous God himself, there might have been reason for this jealousy. But Esau hated Jacob because of the blessing; and now that hatred revived when the blessing was about to be inherited. We must not think it strange if reasonable requests are denied by unreasonable men, and if those whom God favors are affronted by people.

God tells Aaron to prepare to die. There is something of displeasure in these orders. Aaron must not enter Canaan because he failed in his duty at the waters of strife. There is much mercy in them. Aaron, though he dies for his transgression, dies with ease and honor. He is gathered to his people as one who dies in the arms of Divine grace. There is much significance in these orders. Aaron must not enter Canaan to show that the Levitical priesthood could not make anything perfect; that must be done by bringing in a better hope. Aaron submits and dies in the manner appointed, and for all we know, with as much cheerfulness as if he were going to bed. It was a great satisfaction to Aaron to see his son, who was dear to him, preferred and his office preserved and secured; especially, to see in this a representation of Christ’s everlasting priesthood. A good man would desire, if it were the will of God, not to outlive his usefulness. Why should we covet to continue any longer in this world than while we may do some service in it for God and our generation?
